audit_items.proof_trace: `ber_flatten` dereferences `ber->ber_memctx` at line 440 without any null check on `ber`, while its companion `ber_flatten2` explicitly handles `ber == NULL` at line 396 (creating an empty berval), implying the API contract permits NULL — the proof that all dereferences are guarded fails here. Additionally, accessor functions `ber_start` (L693), `ber_len` (L699), `ber_ptrlen` (L705), `ber_rewind` (L711-714), and `ber_remaining` (L724) perform unguarded dereferences with no null checks whatsoever; most other functions rely solely on `assert()` which is stripped in production builds.
audit_items.attack_scenario: An attacker induces memory exhaustion via large or numerous LDAP requests; if `ber_alloc_t()` returns NULL and calling server code omits the return-value check before passing the result to `ber_flatten`, the server dereferences NULL at line 440, crashing the LDAP daemon (remote DoS).
